coredata.dc:title: A novel molecularly imprinted polymer decorated by CQDs@HBNNS nanocomposite and UiO-66-NH2 for ultra-selective electrochemical sensing of Oxaliplatin in biological samples
coredata.dc:creator: Hatamluyi, Behnaz
Hashemzadeh, Alireza
Darroudi, Majid
coredata.dc:description: A platform equipped with a well-designed synthetic interface, as a universal sensor, is required to detect highly selective and sensitive anticancer drugs in complex matrices of different biological samples. In this study, we developed a novel electrochemical sensor based on the combination of molecularly imprinted method with the 3D-framework UiO-66-NH2 as excellent target molecule recognizer on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode modified with carbon quantum dots incorporated hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets (CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2 /MIP/GCE) for ultraselective detection of trace levels of Oxaliplatin (OXA) as an anticancer drug. The increased efficiency and sensitivity of the proposed MIP sensor was obtained due to the use of UiO-66-NH2 with high porosity structure which effectively increased the number of binding sites and specific surface area as well as CQDs@HBNNS nanocomposite (CQDs@HBNNS-NCs) with ideal electric conductivity and synergistic effects to facilitate the electron transfer process. During MIP preparation, optimal conditions were achieved by using experimental design, based on a standard orthogonal array, and different electrodes were characterized by scanning electron microscopy and cyclic voltammetry. Electrochemical responses of imprinted sensor displayed two dynamic linear ranges from 1.0 to 20.0 nM and 20.0 to 250.0 nM with a detection limit of 0.37 nM. In addition, superior anti-interfering ability of the designed MIP sensor toward OXA detection in the presence of interferents at high concentration especially other Pt-based anticancer drugs as well as reproducibility and stability of it proved that the CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2 /MIP/GCE could be extensively applied in the clinical therapeutic drug monitoring.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic preparation of CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE and electrochemical detection of OXA.
(A) TEM image and (B) size distribution of CQDs; (C) and (D) FESEM images of bulk boron nitride and HBNNS; (E) TEM image of CQDs@HBNNS-NCs; (F) FTIR spectra of HBNNS, CQDs, and CQDs@HBNNS-NCs.
(A) FESEM and (B) TEM image of UiO-66-NH2; (C) BET; (D) XRD pattern; (E) FTIR spectra; and (F) DLS analyses of UiO-66-NH2.

(A) CVs of different modified electrodes in solution of 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl, bare GCE (a), CQDs@HBNNS/GCE (b), CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE before (c) and after (d) removing the OXA, CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE after 360 s incubation in 10.0 nM OXA solution (e), and CQDs@HBNNS/MIP/GCE after removing the OXA (f), (B) CV plots of CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1.0 mM) and KCl (0.1 M) solution at different scan rates (from 10 to 400 mV/s), (C) A linear relationship between the redox peak current of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution and the square root of the scan rate, (D) FESEM images of bare GCE, (E) CQDs@HBNNS/GCE, (F) CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE before, and (G) CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE after removing the OXA.

(A) DPVs of CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE at various concentrations of OXA (0, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 60.0, 100.0, 150.0, 200.0, and 250.0 nM), (B) and (C) calibration curves correlating Δi with the concentration of OXA.

Selectivity of CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE electrochemical platform toward OXA (100.0 nM) in the presence of (A) Biomolecules (a: 0, b: 20,000; c: 40,000 and d: 60,000 nM) and (B) Ions (a: 0, e: 50000, f: 70000, g: 100,000 nM).
The selectivity of the imprinted and non-imprinted sensor for OXA and interferences.
Analytical results for DPV measurements in aqueous, pharmaceutical, serum, and urine samples.

head.ce:title: A novel molecularly imprinted polymer decorated by CQDs@HBNNS nanocomposite and UiO-66-NH2 for ultra-selective electrochemical sensing of Oxaliplatin in biological samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A platform equipped with a well-designed synthetic interface, as a universal sensor, is required to detect highly selective and sensitive anticancer drugs in complex matrices of different biological samples. In this study, we developed a novel electrochemical sensor based on the combination of molecularly imprinted method with the 3D-framework UiO-66-NH2 as excellent target molecule recognizer on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode modified with carbon quantum dots incorporated hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets (CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2 /MIP/GCE) for ultraselective detection of trace levels of Oxaliplatin (OXA) as an anticancer drug. The increased efficiency and sensitivity of the proposed MIP sensor was obtained due to the use of UiO-66-NH2 with high porosity structure which effectively increased the number of binding sites and specific surface area as well as CQDs@HBNNS nanocomposite (CQDs@HBNNS-NCs) with ideal electric conductivity and synergistic effects to facilitate the electron transfer process. During MIP preparation, optimal conditions were achieved by using experimental design, based on a standard orthogonal array, and different electrodes were characterized by scanning electron microscopy and cyclic voltammetry. Electrochemical responses of imprinted sensor displayed two dynamic linear ranges from 1.0 to 20.0 nM and 20.0 to 250.0 nM with a detection limit of 0.37 nM. In addition, superior anti-interfering ability of the designed MIP sensor toward OXA detection in the presence of interferents at high concentration especially other Pt-based anticancer drugs as well as reproducibility and stability of it proved that the CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2 /MIP/GCE could be extensively applied in the clinical therapeutic drug monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxaliplatin (OXA) refers to a second-generation of platinum-based chemotherapeutic agents that are derived from the cisplatin [1,2]. The OXA is usually used in combination with other cytostatics for the treatment of colorectal cancer, breast cancer, lung cancer, and ovarian cancer [3]. The antitumor activity of the OXA is related to its interaction with DNA and the formation of a stable toxic drug-DNA adduct, thus interfering with replication and transcription processes [4]. In spite of the great efficacy of OXA compared to other Pt-based drugs, its clinical utility is limited due to some side effects such as nausea and vomiting, neurotoxicity, low blood counts, and neuropathy [5,6]. A high dosage of OXA should be used in some form of cancers leading to high health risks [7]. The OXA could form other reactive mono-/dichloro- or diaqua that can form complexes with DNA, amino acids, or other biomolecules, which may be caused by OXA accumulation in tissues and its cytotoxicity can be attributed to its cellular uptake and accumulation in body tissues [8]. Hence, monitoring OXA deposition inpatients and those handling OXA during and after treatment is of great importance [7]. However, to gain a better understanding of the working mechanism, pharmacology, and pharmacokinetics of OXA, a rapid, sensitive and selective detection strategy is needed for use in the serum or other biological matrices. To date, detection of Pt-based drugs in biological samples is often accomplished by measuring the Pt concentration in aqueous biological media using various analytical methods including spectrophotometry [9], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [10], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [11,12], capillary electrophoresis and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) coupled to a range of detectors [13,14], as well as chemiluminescence (CL) technique [15]. Despite the high sensitivity, methods such as AAS and ICP-MS are incapable of distinguishing a particular Pt-based drug unless coupled to a suitable separation system such as capillary electrophoresis and chromatography. However, chromatography techniques suffer from some disadvantages such as high cost, time-consuming, complicated pretreatment steps, and large instruments. Therefore, finding an efficient, sensitive and selective procedure to detect a specific Pt-based drug among other Pt-based drugs is important to effective treatment in cancer therapy. Electrochemical analysis based on molecularly imprinting method, owing to its excellent merits like specific template recognition, favorable stability at tough conditions, ease of miniaturization, low cost, and less-analyte required, is known as an ideal method for generation of specific sensing signals [16–18]. By coupling electrochemical sensors with MIP methods, highly efficient devices with specific recognition sites towards target molecules are created, which have a wider practical application in the analysis of important compounds [19]. Nevertheless, electrochemical sensors that rely on the use of MIP method as recognition receptors suffer from some problems. Despite having high selectivity for their target molecules, the application of these polymeric networks for the rapid and efficient detection of analytes is limited due to the rigid and dense frameworks resulting poor site accessibility and low-rate mass transfer [20]. One of the most effective strategies to overcome these shortcomings has been the use of new type of crystalline materials, microporous metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), to engineer novel MIP-based electrochemical sensors. Due to the unique properties of MOFs, such as high porosity, appropriate pore volume, tunable pore size, and especially significant surface area, it’s predictable that most of the active sites for electrocatalysis would be created at the surface of the MIP polymeric network in the presence of MOFs [21–26]. As a result, removing and rebounding processes of template molecules can be done easier and faster [27,28]. However, the utilization of single component MOFs as electrocatalysis in the MIP film is challenging due to their poor conductivity and the lack of signal transition capacity [29]. To overcome these drawbacks, nanomaterial have been used as a key component in the family of MIPs [30–32].

Numerous types of nanomaterials, such as carbon-based nanocomposites [33], metal/metal oxide nanoparticles [34], and noble metal nanoparticles [35], have been exploited in conjunction with MIPs to improve conductivity and facilitate the electron transfer rate. Among them, the nanocomposites produced from the incorporation of hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets (HBNNS) with carbon-based nanostructures could be ideal alternatives to enhance the electrical conductivity of the MIP film. For instance, Yola and Atar fabricated an electrochemical sensor based on a glassy carbon electrode modified with 2D-hBN/f-MWCNTs nanocomposite for simultaneous detection of β-agonists with desirable analytical performance [36]. Additionally, Yola and Atar prepared a MIP sensor based on GQDs/2D-hBN nanocomposite for the determination of serotonin, which exhibited a low detection limit of 2.0 × 10−13 M within the range of 1.0 × 10-12 - 1.0 × 10-8 M [37]. HBNNS, which are produced by replacing C atoms in graphene single layer with B and N atoms [38], have superior catalytic activity due to abundant functional groups, large bandgap, good conductivity, and high surface area [39,40]. On the other hand, CQDs are known as an excellent electrode modifier material owing to its exceptional electrocatalytic properties (non-toxicity, surface functional groups, excellent biocompatibility, high conductivity, and low cost) [41–43]. Considering the above mentioned, monodisperse CQDs on HBNNS (CQDs@HBNNS-NCs) significantly increases the performance of electrochemical sensors to synergistic effects.

Accordingly, in the present study, a novel OXA imprinted electrode was constructed based on CQDs@HBNNS-NCs modified GCE (CQDs@HBNNS/GCE) by means of MOF (UiO-66-NH2) incorporated molecular imprinting method which is termed CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE. The purpose of using UiO-66-NH2 as MOF is to introduce another nanosized material with a highly porous structure in the MIP field. UiO-66-NH2 has numerous inherent advantages, e.g., high chemical and hydrothermal stabilities, and high specific surface area [44]. These properties make UiO-66-NH2 an ideal support material in the MIP field. The CQDs@HBNNS-NCs presented good conductivity with large surface area, therefore increasing the sensitivity of the sensor and UiO-66-NH2 with high porosity improving the binding capacity for the MIP film. To achieve the optimal conditions for the construction of CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP, several parameters were carefully studied using the experimental design, based on a standard orthogonal array with Minitab software. As far as we are aware, the MIP sensor based on CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2 for quantitative OXA detection has not been reported. This work is expected to offer an easy and promising procedure for fabrication of electrochemical sensors with excellent sensing performance to quantitative determination of Pt-based drugs.

Electrochemical signals were recorded by an electrochemical analyzer (Autolab PGSTAT 101, Netherlands) equipped with a three-electrode system, that was configured by connecting an Ag/AgCl electrode, a platinum electrode, and a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as a reference electrode, counter electrode, and working electrode, respectively. The images of surface morphology were obtained by a model TESCAN MIRA3-FEG field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), and particle size of the synthesized nanostructures were gained by model ALEO 912 AB transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were studied on Philips's analytical X-ray. Studies of surface area and porosity of synthesized MOF were performed based on Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) by BELSORP-miniII analyzer (Particle Test Pty Ltd, Bel Japan Inc., Japan) using N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77 K in a liquid nitrogen media.

Oxaliplatin, Isopropyl alcohol, 2-aminoterephthalic acid, zirconium tetrachloride, and triethanolamine were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Boron nitride powders, azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), methacrylic acid (MAA), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), citric acid, acetone, ethanol, ammonium hydroxide, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, H3PO4, HCl, and NaOH were obtained from Merck.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs, HBNNS, and CQDs@HBNNS-NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized according to the hydrothermal procedure [45]. The citric acid (5.5 mmol) and triethanolamine (5.0 mmol) were dissolved in double-distilled water (10 mL). The resulting mixture was poured into a Teflon lined stainless autoclave and kept at 200 °C. After 5 h of reactions, a yellow CQDs solution was obtained. The prepared CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C into a dark container. The HBNNS was synthesized following a known method [39]. Solid portions (100.0 mg) of bulk boron nitride were dispersed in 50.0 mL isopropyl alcohol and then stirred at 50 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the dispersion was ultrasonicated for 20 h. After centrifugation, the white sediments were collected and washed with acetone and dried at 60 °C for 24 h to create HBNNS. For preparation CQDs@HBNNS-NCs, HBNNS (10.0 mg/mL) was firstly dispersed in absolute ethanol and ultrasonicated for 30 min to get homogeneous dispersion. After that, the synthesized CQDs and HBNNS homogeneous dispersion were mixed together by the quality ratio of 2:10 (v/v) under continuous stirring for 20 min. Finally, the mixture was heated in a Teflon lined autoclave at 80 °C for 1 h and prepared CQDs@HBNNS-NCs was collected.

UiO-66-NH2 was prepared based on a previously reported procedure [46]. Briefly, 2-aminoterephthalic acid (536 mg, 4 mmol) and anhydrous zirconium tetrachloride (500 mg, 2.16 mmol) were separately dissolved in 60 and 20 mL DMF. To obtain a clear solution, hydrochloric acid (4.0 mL) was added to the latter solution. Both solutions were sonicated for about half an hour. Then, the solution of 2-aminoterephthalic acid was added to the other one and sonicated for another half an hour. After that, the mixture was conveyed to a Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 80 °C for 36 h. The finalized product was separated by centrifugation. The yellow precipitate was rinsed with DMF, water, and ethanol three times.

In the preparation of UiO-66-NH2/MIP, 14.0 mM MAA as the monomer, 7.0 mM OXA as the template, 0.002 mg/mL UiO-66-NH2 as the framework materials were added to 50.0 mL acetonitrile-methanol (2:1 V/V) solution as the solvent and sonicated for 30 min. The dispersed mixture was kept for 24 h at 4 °C. Then, 20.0 mg ABIN (initiator) and 150.0 μL EGDMA (crosslinking agent) were added to 10.0 mL of the above mixture, and it was placed in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. After the removal of dissolved oxygen by purging with nitrogen for 20 min, the polymerization was performed by stirring at 70 °C for 24 h in an oil bath under a nitrogen atmosphere. When the reaction was completed, the template molecules were extracted through several times washing of MIP particles with 0.20 M NaOH solution until no OXA was detected by UV–vis spectrometry. They were then further rinsed with ethanol and double distilled water and finally dried under vacuum at 60 °C. To evaluate the efficiency of the UiO-66-NH2/MIP in OXA detection, the non-imprinted polymer (NIP), was also prepared parallel to the UiO-66-NH2/MIP under the above conditions except the participation of OXA molecules.

Firstly, the glassy carbon working electrode was polished using alumina powder (0.05 μm) and then ultrasonic cleaned by a mixture of ethanol: distilled water solution (1:1, v/v), and dried in air. Next, an aliquot of 15.0 μL of the prepared CQDs@HBNNS-NCs was carefully coated on the clean surface of the GCE, and then, it was dried under infrared light. Chitosan was dissolved (3 % w/w) in acetic acid aqueous solution (3 % v/v), then 10.0 mg of the prepared UiO-66-NH2/MIP was dispersed in 1.0 mL of the solution. Finally, 5.0 μL of UiO-66-NH2/MIP suspensions were dropped onto the CQDs@HBNNS/GCE and dried at 60 °C to obtain CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE. The whole preparation procedure of the CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

In this work, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was selected as a redox probe and electrochemical performance of different modified sensors was evaluated by studying the change in the electrochemical signal of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− by cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique. The concentration of OXA was also determined indirectly by measuring the intensity of the peak current of a redox probe by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). For this, the DPV of 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe was firstly recorded in 0.1 M KCl as a supporting electrolyte in the potential range from -0.2 to +0.6 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s by prepared MIP sensor. Then the sensor was incubated in a solution containing appropriate concentration of OXA for 360 s, and after washing with distilled water, it was placed in probe solution and again the corresponding DP voltammogram of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- was recorded. Quantitative measurement of OXA was done based on the difference (Δi) of the peak current of redox probe in the absence and in the presence of the OXA molecules. In order to retrieve the sensor for next analysis, the CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE was placed in 0.20 M NaOH solution and CV was done between -1.00 V and +1.00 V until a stable redox peak can be obtained in [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution.

Injection solution form of OXA, human blood serum and urine samples were chosen as real samples to evaluate the practicability of CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE to determine OXA in pharmaceutical compounds and biological sample matrices. At first, human blood serum and urine samples (obtained from a local medical diagnostic laboratory) were pre-treated by centrifugation (3500 rpm for 10 min). Then a 0.5 mL aliquot of the prepared samples were spiked with known concentrations of OXA standard stock solutions and diluted 100 times with phosphate buffer (PB, 0.1 M) to give a series of OXA solutions. Also, the solutions with different concentrations of OXA injection solution were prepared by diluting with PB, without any pretreatment. To achieve the highest accuracy of the results, all experiments were done using the standard addition method, and each experiment was repeated six times.

The TEM image and size distribution of the CQDs are presented in Fig. 1
A. The CQDs exhibited high monodispersity and almost spherical shape. According to the size distribution histogram (Fig. 1 B), ranges of CQDs were 3.0–8.0 nm, and the average diameter of them was 5.5 nm. To ensure the formation of HBNNSs, as well as study of their morphology, FESEM images of the bulk boron nitride and synthesized HBNNSs, were prepared. In Fig. 1 C, which is related to the bulk boron nitride, irregular morphology and different dimensions of boron nitride can be observed, while in Fig. 1 D, folded and flat nanosheets are clearly seen. The comparison of Fig. 1 C and D confirm that the bulk boron nitride is successfully converted into ultrathin HBNNS. The morphology of the prepared CQDs@HBNNS-NCs was also studied using the TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 E, the HBNNS has a pellucid film-like structure, which CQDs are distributed homogeneously and stick to the surface of it. The FTIR spectra of HBNNSs, CQDs, and CQDs@HBNNS-NCs are displayed in Fig. 1 F. The FTIR signal of HBNNSs near 3440 cm−1 is assigned to the B-(OH) group stretching mode [40], and the peaks at 1410 cm−1, 799 cm−1, and 510 cm−1 are respectively related to the B–N, B-N-B, and OBO bands [38]. For CQDs, the peaks at 3418 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of OH and CO, while the peaks at 1381 cm−1 and 1038 cm−1 can be assigned to the COC asymmetric coupled vibrations and symmetric coupled stretching, respectively [47]. The characteristic peaks of the B-(OH), B-N, B-N-B, CO, and COC groups are well found in the CQDs@HBNNS-NCs, indicating the successful association between CQDs and HBNNSs.
The synthesized UiO-66-NH2 was also fully characterized by different characterization methods, including FESEM, TEM, BET, XRD, FTIR, DLS, and ξ-potential, and the results were explained.

The FESEM and TEM images illustrated the uniformity of the size and shape of the synthesized UiO-66-NH2, which were spherical and 73 nm in diameter (Fig. 2
A and B). The BET analysis revealed the surface area of 715 m2/g and a pore size of 12 Å (Fig. 2 C). The structure formation was confirmed by XRD pattern, which was compatible with powder pattern obtained from crystal information file (Ref. # RUBTAK02) (Fig. 2 D) [48]. The FTIR spectra exhibited two peaks at 3465 and 3408 cm−1, which were related to the asymmetrical and symmetrical vibration of amine groups in 2-aminoterephthalic acid. The symmetrical and asymmetrical vibration of carboxylic acids appeared at 1400−1650 cm−1 region. Some other peaks under 1400 cm−1 were also related to the ligand, especially the nitrogen and aromatic carbon (Fig. 2 E) [49]. The DLS analysis was displayed size of 151.6 nm in water and ξ-potential of 21.2 mV. (Fig. 2 F).

The electrical conductivity as an important characteristic of the modified electrode was studied in 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl solution by CV technique at various stages of modification. The CV profiles of the bare GCE and different modified electrodes are presented in Fig. 3
A. As demonstrated, bare GCE displays a pair of reversible redox peaks for [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (curve a). After the decoration of CQDs@HBNNS-NCs on GCE, a remarkable increase was observed in the oxidation/reduction peak current of potassium ferricyanide due to improved conductivity and enlarged effective surface area (curve b). For electrode modified with MIP materials without removal of the template molecules, the entry of ferrocyanide anions into the electrode surface was difficult due to the poor electron transferability of the compact membrane [50]. As a result, the intensity of the electrochemical signals decreased significantly (curve c). By removing template molecules from the MIP matrix, the current responses sharply enhanced, which showed that the diffusion of probe molecules to the electrode surface was facilitated through the produced cavities (curve d). At last, incubation of the electrode in OXA solution for a specific time, caused the decrease of the redox peak (curve e) compared with curve d, signifying that OXA molecules occupied some of the imprinting cavities and blocked the entrance channel of the probe molecules. To explore the effect of UiO-66-NH2 on sensor performance, the CV profile of MIP electrode prepared in the absence of the UiO-66-NH2 was recorded after removal of the template molecules. It was observed that the CQDs@HBNNS/MIP/GCE offered weak oxidation/reduction peak current (curve f) compared to curve d, which demonstrates the fact that MOF with high porosity and increased surface area create many active sites on the MIP network and accelerate transport of electrons [51]. These results proved that OXA-imprinted MIP film has been successfully formed on the electrode surface and the designed CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE was highly sensitive and selective to OXA.

To investigate the electrochemical mechanism of the MIP sensor, CVs were recorded at various scanning rates between 10 and 400 mV/s in potassium ferricyanide (1.0 mM) and KCl (1.0 M) solution. As shown in Fig. 3 B, both anodic and cathodic peak currents enhanced linearly with increasing scan rate. The linear dependence between the redox peak currents and the square root of the scan rate with regression Eqs. (1) and (2) proved that electrochemical redox reaction of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE is a diffusion-controlled mechanism (Fig. 3 C). The regression equations are as follows:
(1) Ipa = 0.00012 + 0.00074 (v)1/2 (V/s)1/2, R2 = 0.9994
(2) Ipc = −0.00010 – 0.00070 (v)1/2 (V/s)1/2, R2 = 0.9935

The active surface area of different electrodes was also computed based on Randles - Sevcik equation [52] as 0.228 ± 0.002, 0.941 ± 0.002, 0.811 ± 0.004, and 0.538 ± 0.003 cm2 for bare GCE, CQDs@HBNNS/GCE, CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE, and CQDs@HBNNS/MIP/GCE. It should be noted that the experiments for calculating the effective surface area of the electrodes were conducted repeatedly, and then each result was expressed as the average value of five reduplicate experiments.

To further confirm the formation of CQDs@HBNNS-NCs and MIP film on the surface of the electrode, the morphological changes of the unmodified and modified electrodes were studied by FESEM. As shown in Fig. 3 D, the surface of bare GCE is smooth. After decorating the electrode surface with CQDs@HBNNS-NCs, it is observed that the whole surface is coated uniformly with the boron nitride nanosheets, which the CQDs are placed on them (Fig. 3 E). After MIP deposition (Fig. 3 F), the surface of the electrode coated with the intensive polymer layer, indicating that the CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE was successfully prepared. Moreover, after extraction of OXA (Fig.3 G), the polymer membrane became rougher than before, and a large number of cavities appeared at its surface, which can be attributed to the effective removal of OXA molecules.

It is evident that the optimization step of the experimental conditions is one of the most important steps in the construction of the sensor since it directly affects the performance of the sensor. In this study, the best conditions for the preparation of MIP were obtained using the Taguchi standard orthogonal array methodology [53]. Our pre-testing experiments showed that four key factors, including the molar ratio of OXA to MAA, the mass ratio of OXA to UiO-66-NH2, the amount of EGDMA, and the amount of AIBN should be optimized in five levels (Tab. S1). Based on L25 orthogonal array, the effect of selected parameters was studied by performing twenty-five experiments. In this series of trials Δi (the peak current obtained for ferricyanide in the absence of 10.0 nM template molecules minus the peak current recorded in the presence of template molecules at CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE) in DPV technique was used as the responses of experiments and the signal to noise (S/N) ratio was used to determine the optimal level of each factor [54]. Taguchi L25 orthogonal array, the average of the responses obtained from the triple repetition of each run with a deviation of less than 5 %, and the ratio of S/N are given in Tab. S2. The estimated effects index based on the delta values (Δ = max – min), indicated that the mass ratio of OXA to UiO66-NH2 had a main effect on sensor performance (Tab. S3). This is because the UiO-66-NH2 has a large area and permanent holes; thus, it will promote the process of mass transfer through its porous structure. The optimal level of each factor can be determined from Fig. S1. Detailed information on selecting the best conditions for the preparation of MIP is provided in the Supplementary Material. To confirm and validate the experimental design, analysis of variance (ANOVA) was also performed [55]. The results of ANOVA are shown in Tab. S4. These results are in good agreement with the results of Tab. S3 and Fig. S1. According to the performed experiments and the obtained results, the following conditions were selected as the optimum obtained MIP [Molar ratio of OXA/MAA is 1:2; Mass ratio of OXA/UiO66-NH2 is 1:0.75; and using of EGDMA (150 μL) and AIBN (20.0 mg)].

Moreover, the influence of several other critical factors (i.e., pH, incubation time, and amounts of CQDs@HBNNS-NCs and MIP) were also investigated on sensor efficiency. The influence of the pH on the template molecule rebinding was studied in the pH range from 3.0 to 10.0 in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.10 M) by DPV measurements. As shown in Fig. S2(A), the maximum Δi was obtained in pH 7.0. Therefore, it was considered as an optimal pH that facilitates the interaction of OXA molecules with the recognition cavities.

The incubation time is defined as the required equilibrium time for the uptake of analyte into the created holes. Consequently, the bonding capacity of the template molecules to the surface of the electrode and the sensitivity of the sensor is directly dependent on the incubation time. In the present study, the CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE was incubated in 10.0 nM OXA solution containing 0.10 M PBS (pH ̴ 7.0) for various time periods. From Fig. S2 (B), it can be realized that the values of Δi enhanced with increasing incubation time from 60 to 360 s and then it remained almost constant. It might be due to the saturation of the OXA on the surface of the electrode. Accordingly, 360 s was selected as optimum incubation time. To find out the effect of the amount of CQDs@HBNNS-NCs on the electrochemical response of MIP electrode, different amounts of CQDs@HBNNS-NCs were used in the construction process. As presented in Fig. S2 (C), when the amount of nanocomposite increased from 2.0 to 15.0 μL, the Δi increased as well. This is because the increased amount of nanocomposite, with its unique properties, improved electron transfer rate, and the current response. Consequently, 15.0 μL of CQDs@HBNNS-NCs was deemed the optimum amount.

Finally, the effect of MIP film thickness on the recognition ability of the imprinting sensor was investigated, and experimental results showed that the maximum Δi was obtained when 5.0 μL MIP was used (Fig. S2 (D)). Reducing the current response in high MIP amounts can be attributed to reducing the conductivity of the electrode surface. Because the increase in the amount of MIP causes the polymer film to thicken and prevents the entry of template molecules into imprinted portion. Therefore, an optimized MIP amount of 5.0 μL was selected.

The sensing ability of CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE to OXA was studied in view of linearity range, detection limit, and spike recovery in aqueous solution and real samples (pharmaceuticals compounds, blood serum, and urine), under optimized experimental conditions. The recorded DPV signals in Fig. 4
A reveals that after the incubation of the MIP electrode at various concentrations of the OXA solution, the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− response current gradually decreased with the increase of OXA concentration. Excellent linear dependency between Δi and OXA concentration was found over two ranges from 1.0 to 20.0 nM and 20.0 to 250.0 nM with detection limit of 0.37 nM (based on 3 Sb/m, six times of the standard deviation of the blank divided by the slope of the calibration plot) in aqueous solution [56] (Fig. 4 B and C). Similar experiments were also carried out on the matrices of real samples. The linear regression equations, quantitative OXA recoveries, and detection limits in aqueous solution, as well as real sample matrices, were presented in Table 1 . These results reflect the similar behavior of the electrode in the aqueous solution and the real sample matrices, and it is well established that the CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE has valuable and acceptable potential for analysis of OXA in real sample matrices.

The performance of the constructed sensor has been compared with that of several reported techniques for OXA detection. Based on the data of Table 2 , the CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE is a promising platform for application in the monitoring of OXA in complicated matrices.

Various biomolecules like ascorbic acid, uric acid, and glucose as well as several ions like
Na +
,

,

2 +
,

,

,

,

, and

2 -
are commonly found in biological sample matrices. Therefore, specific recognition ability of CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE toward OXA was studied in the presence of these interfering species, and the corresponding results are given in Fig. 5 . Under the optimal conditions, DPV responses of the proposed MIP sensor were recorded after incubation of the electrode in the 100.0 nM OXA solution containing different concentrations of interfering species. Based on the results, only slight signal changes (< 5 %) were found in Δi values in the presence of 60,000 nM uric acid, ascorbic acid, and glucose and 100,000 nM of ions. The physiological levels of investigated biomolecules/ions are presented in Tab. S5 [60].

Also, to further confirm the anti-interference ability of the developed MIP sensor, the responses of the MIP and NIP electrodes were studied in 10.0 nM OXA coexisting with several platinum-based anticancer drugs like cisplatin, carboplatin, and picoplatin, as platinum (II) drugs as well as satraplatin, as platinum (IV) drug and imprinting factor
I . F . =
Δ i
M I P
Δ i
N I P
were also calculated for them. The results showed that the presence of 6 equiv. of platinum-based drugs had a negligible influence on Δi values in addition, according to Fig. 6 , the I.F. values were calculated for OXA, cisplatin, carboplatin, picoplatin, and satraplatin as 15.49, 1.03, 1.09, 1.13, and 1.38, respectively, indicating the superior anti-interference ability of the established MIP sensor for selective detection of the OXA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method validation and stability of the CQDs@HBNNS/UiO-66-NH2/MIP/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability of the proposed sensor was studied based on the intraday precision and interday precision by measuring the DPV response of a 10.0 nM OXA solution using a single electrode for 6 times. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for intraday and interday experiments were 2.15 % and 2.29 %, respectively, which proved that the repeatability of the MIP sensor was satisfactory. To evaluate the reproducibility of the proposed sensor DPV measurements were done for detection of 10.0 nM OXA solution using six independently electrodes, which were fabricated under the same conditions. The results indicated that the variation of Δi remains stable for different MIP sensors, giving the RSD of 2.47 % (Fig. S3 (A)). To estimate the stability, the imprinted electrode was stored at 4 °C and measured OXA (10.0 nM) every three days. The electrochemical signal change of the electrode was measured over a period of four weeks and observed that 93.7 % of the initial response was retained (Fig. S3 (B)).

For the first time, a novel detecting system was developed for ultra-selective and sensitive sensing of OXA in aqueous/real samples based on CQDs@HBNNS-NCs and 3D-framework UiO-66-NH2 combined with MIP. The nanosized CQDs@HBNNS-NCs increase the conductivity and effective surface area of the sensing system, and UiO-66-NH2 with microporous structure enhance the binding capacity. According to the obtained LODs in aqueous solution (0.37 nM), pharmaceutical (0.40 nM), blood serum (0.39 nM), and urine sample (0.41 nM), as well as extraordinary imprinting factor (I.F. = 15.49) it could be stated that the proposed sensing platform is highly specific and sensitive. Furthermore, the developed electrochemical sensor showed excellent repeatability, reproducibility, and stability. The results presented in this work are promising and show the potential of this novel strategy for rapid, sensitive, and selective determination of OXA in clinical application. However, given the prominent role of platinum-based anticancer drugs in the treatment of various tumor diseases, especially the simultaneous use of them in different treatment regimens, we believe that research efforts must be continued to fabricate electrochemical sensors for the measurement of several platinum-based anticancer drugs simultaneously.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Behnaz Hatamluyi, a postdoctoral researcher in the Pharmacological Research Center of Medicinal Plants at Mashhad University of Medical Sciences. Her research interests include the development of electrochemical sensors and biosensors based on different nanomaterials.

Alireza Hashemzadeh, a postdoctoral researcher in the nanoscience center at Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, the specialist in design and synthesis of new inorganic nanoparticles, porous and mesoporous materials for different purposes. He has 14 published works in this regard.

Majid Darroudi, assistant professor of Nanosciences at Mashhad University of Medical Sciences with research interests in green synthesis of different nanomaterials and investigation of their applications in medicine and pharmacy, published 120+ peer-reviewed international papers with 3 chapter books in both fields.
